title: FAANG Layoff Survival Guide
description: FAANG layoffs are not like SAP restructuring. At-will employment, RIFs (Reduction in Force), severance negotiation, and the emotional whiplash of losing access in 30 minutes are realities that SAP engineers are not prepared for. This guide covers building a layoff-proof portfolio, navigating the layoff process, severance negotiation, emotional recovery, networking during downturns, and knowing when to vest-and-leave before the layoff hits.

sections.title: The FAANG Layoff Reality — At-Will Employment and RIFs

description.value:
If you come from SAP (especially SAP Labs or a permanent SAP position), the concept of sudden termination is foreign. SAP restructures with months of notice, severance packages that improve with tenure, and a general sense of stability. FAANG does not work this way. Every FAANG employment offer states, in bold or capital letters, that employment is 'at-will' — you can be terminated at any time, with or without cause, with or without notice. This is not theoretical. It happens to thousands of engineers every year.

How FAANG layoffs actually work: (1) The company announces a RIF (Reduction in Force) — usually in an all-hands email from the CEO. The announcement happens at 6-7 AM Pacific time. Within 2 hours, affected employees lose access to all systems: email, Slack, GitHub, build systems, VPN. (2) You receive a calendar invite for a 15-minute meeting with your manager and HR. The meeting starts with: 'Your position has been eliminated.' (3) You are given a severance document to sign. You have 21 days to review (under federal law, if you're over 40). You may have a few days to return equipment. (4) Your Slack account goes dark. Your email auto-responds. You are gone from the internal directory within 24 hours.

The most important thing to understand: Layoffs are not performance reviews. They are not personal. RIFs are determined by business unit, org, team, and role — not individual performance. The highest-rated engineer on a team being eliminated gets laid off. The lowest-rated engineer on a retained team stays. This is the opposite of the performance-based termination that SAP engineers are used to. Internalizing this distinction is critical for emotional recovery: 'I was laid off because my team's project was deprioritized, not because I failed.'

The FAANG layoff math: As of 2024-2026, the typical FAANG layoff affects 5-15% of the company. In a company of 50,000 engineers, that's 2,500-7,500 people. At the Staff+ level (L6/E6/L64+), layoff rates are typically lower than at junior levels — senior engineers are more expensive but harder to replace. However, entire orgs can be eliminated. In 2022-2023, Meta eliminated entire product orgs (including dozens of Staff+ engineers). Google eliminated the entire Stadia team. No one is safe by title or level.

sections.codeBlock:
# FAANG Layoff Timeline (Real Example)

## Day 1 (Tuesday)
06:00 AM PT — CEO email: "Reducing workforce by 10%"
06:15 AM PT — Slack goes offline for affected employees
07:00 AM PT — Calendar invites sent for "Check-in" meetings
08:30 AM PT — First round of 1:1 meetings start
09:00 AM PT — Access restored for 30 min to download personal files
09:30 AM PT — All access revoked
10:00 AM PT — Severance documents emailed to personal email
10:00 AM PT — Equipment return instructions emailed

## Day 2-21
Review severance agreement (21 days for age 40+)
Consult with lawyer
Negotiate severance (if possible)

## Day 22
Severance signed or expired
Last day of official employment
COBRA enrollment window opens

description.value:
You cannot prevent a layoff from happening to your org. But you can position yourself so that when the RIF list is made, your name is less likely to appear. Layoff decisions are made by executives who do not know you personally. They look at org charts, project priorities, and headcount allocations. The question they ask: 'If we eliminate this team or reduce its headcount by 20%, what is the business impact?' Your goal is to maximize the perceived business impact of keeping you.

Strategy 1: Own a critical system with high switching cost. The most layoff-proof engineers are the ones who own systems that (a) generate revenue, (b) are hard to replace, or (c) cannot be paused. Examples: payment pipeline, core search ranking, authentication service, data pipeline that feeds the ML models. If you are the only person (or one of 2-3 people) who understands the payment reconciliation system, eliminating you means accepting payment risk. Executives will think twice. Warning: do not make yourself a single point of failure intentionally — that is bus-factor, not job security. But if your natural expertise aligns with a high-criticality system, the layoff protection is a side benefit.

Strategy 2: Work on a high-priority initiative. Every quarter, the company has 3-5 top priorities (usually tied to revenue or strategic bets). Work on one of them. When the RIF comes, the VPs and Directors who decide which teams to cut will protect the teams working on the CEO's top 5 priorities. The team working on 'migrating the legacy alerting system' is more vulnerable than the team working on 'AI-powered ad targeting' — even if the alerting migration is more technically important.

Strategy 3: Be visible to the right people. The Director and VP above you should know who you are and what you do. Not because you self-promote constantly, but because your work is relevant to their priorities. Present at the engineering review. Write a design doc that the VP reads and comments on. Deliver a project that the Director mentions in the weekly staff meeting. When the RIF list is reviewed at the Director level, an anonymous list of names is far easier to approve than a list of people the Director has met and respects.

Strategy 4: Diversify your impact across orgs. If your impact is entirely within one team, your layoff risk is your team's risk. If you have design influence across 3 teams, your risk is partially insulated. Cross-team dependencies, shared libraries, and cross-org initiatives create a network of stakeholders who would feel the loss if you were eliminated. The more stakeholders who would escalate to save you, the safer you are.

decisionTree.prompt: How layoff-proof is your current position?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: What best describes the system or initiative you primarily work on?

branches.path: Strong position. You have natural layoff protection because your system directly impacts revenue. However, don't become complacent — ensure you've documented your system well and have at least one backup person. If your entire team is eliminated, your system still needs to run, and someone needs to understand it.

branches.condition: High-priority strategic initiative (CEO top 5, AI/ML, new product launch)
branches.path: Strong near-term position. Your project is protected as long as it remains a priority. The risk: priorities shift. In 2 quarters, your initiative may no longer be top-5. Make sure you build relationships outside the initiative, and document your impact in business terms (not just technical terms) so you can pivot to another priority.

Moderate risk. Internal projects are harder to justify when headcount is being cut. 'We need this migration' is a weaker argument than 'This migration unlocks $10M in revenue.' Frame your project in business terms. Quantify the cost of not doing it. Build allies on revenue-generating teams who depend on your infrastructure.

branches.condition: Maintenance or legacy system with no growth trajectory
branches.path: Highest risk. If you are maintaining a system that is not growing and not generating direct revenue, you are in the most vulnerable position. Start looking for opportunities to move to a growth initiative. Your best layoff protection is changing your portfolio before the RIF announcement.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The most layoff-proof engineers combine multiple strategies: they own a critical system (Strategy 1) that is part of a high-priority initiative (Strategy 2), present to the VP quarterly (Strategy 3), and have stakeholders in 3+ orgs (Strategy 4). If you have only one of these, you are vulnerable. If you have 3+, you are about as safe as anyone can be in an at-will employment environment.

sections.title: The Layoff Process — What Actually Happens

description.value:
Most engineers learn they've been laid off through the same notification system: an all-hands email from the CEO, followed by a calendar invite for a 15-minute meeting. This section covers what happens minute-by-minute, so you are not caught off guard.

Before the announcement: There are almost always signals. (1) Hiring freeze — if the company pauses hiring, a layoff is likely within 2-4 months. (2) Budget cuts — if your VP announces travel freeze, contractor reductions, or 'efficiency initiatives,' these are precursors. (3) Restructuring — if your org is being reorganized, some teams will be eliminated. (4) Stock price decline — while not directly linked, sustained stock decline often triggers cost-cutting. (5) Peer company layoffs — if other FAANG companies are doing RIFs, yours is likely considering it. Watch for these signals and prepare.

The moment of notification: The meeting will start with HR or your manager saying: 'Your position has been eliminated as part of a company-wide reduction in force.' You will receive: (1) A severance document. (2) A description of benefits continuation (COBRA, insurance). (3) Information about equipment return. (4) A timeline: when your last paycheck is, when insurance ends, when you need to sign the severance agreement. What to say in the meeting: Listen. Take notes. Ask: 'Can you send me the details in writing?' Do NOT argue, negotiate, or vent in this meeting. The decision is final. Save your negotiation energy for the severance discussion.

The 30-minute access window: Most FAANG companies give affected employees 30 minutes to download personal files after the notification. Before this window opens: (1) Do NOT save confidential company code, documents, or intellectual property — this is illegal and can result in legal action. (2) Do save: contact information for colleagues (personal email, LinkedIn, phone), performance reviews you authored, recommendation letters, personal projects you contributed to (with permission). (3) Do NOT: email your team, post in Slack, or make farewell announcements. Your access will be cut mid-sentence. The silence will be jarring.

After access is cut: You will feel a sense of disorientation. Your entire professional identity — the Slack channels, the design docs, the code reviews — disappears instantly. This is normal. Process it. The first 48 hours are for emotional recovery, not job applications.

sections.codeBlock:
# Pre-Layoff Preparation Checklist

## Do NOW (before any layoff) — stored on personal device
- [ ] Export LinkedIn connections from work email
- [ ] Save colleague personal contact info (phone, personal email)
- [ ] Download performance reviews (self + any you wrote for reports)
- [ ] Back up portfolio of work: design docs, tech talks, project summaries
- [ ] Save recommendation letters (if you have them)
- [ ] Update personal GitHub with public contributions
- [ ] Document your role in key projects (for future resume/interviews)
- [ ] Build a "brag doc" — a running list of accomplishments

## Do DURING the 30-min access window
- [ ] Download personal contacts (colleagues, mentors, managers)
- [ ] Save 3-5 recent design docs you authored (if permitted)
- [ ] Download performance review history
- [ ] Do NOT copy source code or confidential documents
- [ ] Do NOT email blast the team

## Do NOT do
- DO NOT sign severance agreement immediately
- DO NOT vent on social media or blind
- DO NOT bad-mouth the company or manager
- DO NOT make farewell posts until you've controlled the narrative

sections.title: Severance and the Negotiation Window

description.value:
Most engineers sign their severance agreement within 48 hours of being laid off. This is a mistake. Federal law gives you 21 days to review (if you are over 40) and 7 days to revoke after signing. FAANG companies expect you to negotiate. The severance package is a starting point, not a final offer.

What severance typically includes: (1) Base pay continuation — 2-4 months of salary, sometimes up to 6 months for long-tenured employees. (2) Equity acceleration — unvested RSUs may vest immediately for a portion (typically the next quarterly vest). (3) COBRA coverage — 3-6 months of paid health insurance continuation. (4) Career support — access to outplacement services (resume review, coaching). (5) Pro-rated bonus — any bonus earned but not yet paid.

What you can negotiate: (1) Extended COBRA coverage — health insurance is the most expensive thing to lose. Ask for 6-12 months of paid COBRA. The company's cost is minimal compared to your cost. (2) Extended vesting — if you were 2 weeks away from a vesting cliff, ask for it to be accelerated. (3) Outplacement services upgrade — some companies offer basic outplacement as default; ask for the premium package. (4) Equipment purchase — ask to buy your laptop at a discount (sometimes they let you keep it). (5) Reference guarantee — ask for a written commitment that the company will provide a positive reference for a specific period (12-24 months). (6) Non-disparagement — ensure the severance agreement protects you if you say something negative, or negotiate for mutual non-disparagement.

The negotiation script: 'I appreciate the severance package. I have a few requests that would make this transition significantly smoother: (1) Can you extend COBRA coverage from 3 months to 6? (2) I was 3 weeks away from my next vesting cliff. Can you accelerate that vest? (3) I would like a written reference commitment for the next 12 months. Would these be possible?' The worst they can say is no. Most FAANG companies have a standard 'deny first' policy — if you don't ask, you leave money on the table.

When to hire a lawyer: If you are over 40 (you have additional legal protections), if you believe the layoff was discriminatory, or if your severance is worth $100K+ (Staff+ level), hire an employment lawyer. The cost ($500-$2K) is worth it. Lawyers know what is standard and what is negotiable. They often save you 2-3x their fee.

The signing timeline: Day 1-7: Do nothing. Process the layoff. Consult with a lawyer. Day 8-14: Negotiate. Send your requests in writing. Day 15-21: Accept the best offer. Sign. Day 22: Move on.

sections.codeBlock:
# Severance Negotiation Script

## Step 1: Acknowledge (in writing, within 48 hours)
"Thank you for the severance documentation. I have received
and am reviewing it. I will respond within the review period.
I have a few questions and requests I'd like to discuss."

## Step 2: Request (day 7-10)
"After reviewing the severance agreement, I would like to
request the following adjustments:

1. COBRA coverage extension from 3 months to 6 months
2. Accelerated vesting of my next quarterly RSU tranche
   (scheduled for [date])
3. A written reference commitment valid for 12 months

I believe these adjustments are reasonable and would
significantly reduce the disruption to my family and
career transition. I am prepared to sign promptly upon
agreement on these terms."

## Step 3: Accept (day 14-21)
"Thank you for agreeing to [extended COBRA / accelerated
vesting / reference commitment]. I have signed the revised
severance agreement and am returning it today."

## Pro tip
Always ask for more than you expect to get.
The first 'no' is the starting price, not the final price.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "I'm over 40 and my severance agreement includes a standard release of claims. Should I sign it?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "You should review it and if it seems fair, sign it." (misses the critical legal distinction for age 40+ employees)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "The Older Workers Benefit Protection Act (OWBPA) gives you specific rights if you are over 40: (1) You have 21 days to review the agreement (not the standard 7). (2) You have 7 days to revoke after signing. (3) The company must disclose the demographics of who was laid off — job titles, ages, and departments. This disclosure helps you assess whether the RIF had a disparate impact on older workers. Do NOT sign before the 21 days. Use that time to: (a) request and review the OWBPA demographic data, (b) consult with an employment lawyer who specializes in age discrimination, (c) negotiate. If the demographic data shows a pattern of laying off older workers disproportionately, you have leverage to negotiate a significantly better severance or to file a legal challenge."
adversarialResponse.pattern: legal-rights-framing — educate about specific legal protections (OWBPA for age 40+, ADEA, WARN Act) that give negotiation leverage. Most engineers don't know their rights, and companies exploit this ignorance.

sections.title: The Emotional and Career Recovery

description.value:
Getting laid off from FAANG is not like getting laid off from a normal company. The identity shock is deeper. You were 'a Google engineer' — a label that carried status, validation, and identity. Losing that label in 30 minutes is a psychological wound that takes time to heal. This section covers the emotional recovery process so you don't make career decisions from a place of hurt.

Phase 1: Shock and denial (days 1-3). You will feel numb, disoriented, and in disbelief. You might check Slack or email out of habit for days. You might feel physical symptoms: loss of appetite, insomnia, fatigue. What to do: (1) Do not apply for jobs. Your judgment is impaired. (2) Do not post on LinkedIn or Blind. Anything you say will be public forever. (3) Tell your closest friends and family. Do not isolate. (4) Exercise, sleep, eat normally. Treat this as a physical recovery, not just an emotional one. (5) If you have a therapist, call them. If you don't, consider scheduling a few sessions — short-term therapy after a layoff is one of the best investments you can make.

Phase 2: Grief and anger (days 4-14). You will cycle through anger ('I gave them 3 years of my life'), self-doubt ('Was my performance bad?'), and fear ('How will I pay my mortgage?'). This is normal. What to do: (1) Separate facts from emotions. Fact: you were laid off because your role was eliminated. Emotion: you feel rejected. Acknowledge both. (2) Talk to other laid-off engineers. Join a WhatsApp group or Slack community of former coworkers. Shared experience normalizes the pain. (3) Start a simple routine: wake up at a normal time, shower, go for a walk, work on one small task. Structure prevents spiraling. (4) Do NOT compare yourself to colleagues who kept their jobs. Their survival was luck, not merit.

Phase 3: Acceptance and action (days 15-30). The fog lifts. You start to see this as an opportunity rather than a catastrophe. What to do: (1) Update your resume and LinkedIn — but not with bitterness. Remove the company name from your headline if you need to. Frame your departure as 'role eliminated in company-wide restructuring.' (2) Activate your network. Message 10-20 people: 'Hi [name], as you may have heard, I was impacted by the RIF. I'm starting to think about next steps. Would you be open to a 15-minute chat in the next week?' (3) Apply to 5-10 roles per week — not 50. Quality over quantity. (4) Consider the possibility of a break. If you have savings, a 4-8 week sabbatical before starting the job search can be transformative.

The FAANG layoff paradox: In the first month, you feel like your career is over. In reality, FAANG engineers laid off in the 2022-2023 wave were rehired at other top companies within 2-5 months on average. The demand for Senior+ engineers did not disappear — it shifted. Companies that were not doing layoffs (Apple, NVIDIA, Microsoft in some divisions) hired aggressively. The paradox: being laid off from FAANG is a credential, not a stigma. Every recruiter knows that RIFs are about budget, not performance.

sections.codeBlock:
# 30-Day Emotional Recovery Plan

## Week 1 (Shock)
- [ ] Tell 3 closest people (family, partner, best friend)
- [ ] Sleep 8+ hours per night
- [ ] Exercise 3x (even a 20-min walk counts)
- [ ] Do NOT check LinkedIn, Blind, or job boards
- [ ] Do NOT sign severance agreement yet
- [ ] One non-career activity per day (cook, hike, read fiction)

## Week 2 (Processing)
- [ ] Talk to 3+ former coworkers who were also laid off
- [ ] Schedule 1 session with a therapist (if not already seeing one)
- [ ] Write a "what I learned" document — no bitterness, just lessons
- [ ] Review and negotiate severance (days 8-14)
- [ ] Set a simple daily schedule (wake, eat, walk, work, sleep)
- [ ] One career-adjacent activity: update resume draft

## Week 3-4 (Re-entry)
- [ ] Finalize and sign severance
- [ ] Update LinkedIn with professional framing
- [ ] Message 10-20 people in your network
- [ ] Apply to 5-10 roles (not 50)
- [ ] Consider: do I want to take a break before searching?
- [ ] First informational interview scheduled

## Ongoing
- [ ] Weekly therapy or coaching session (4-8 weeks recommended)
- [ ] Track applications in a spreadsheet (not in your head)
- [ ] Celebrate small wins: one application, one networking call
- [ ] Limit news/social media to 30 min per day

sections.title: Networking During a Downturn

description.value:
Networking when you are laid off feels desperate. It is not. Layoffs are a known quantity in tech — every senior engineer has either been laid off or worked with someone who has. The key is how you frame the conversation.

The laid-off networking script: 'Hi [name], hope you're doing well. I was impacted by the [Company] RIF — my role was eliminated as part of the restructuring. I'm starting to explore next steps and would love your perspective. Would you be open to a 15-minute chat in the next week or two?' This works because: (1) It is honest but not emotional. (2) It frames the ask as 'perspective' not 'job.' People love giving advice. (3) It is low-commitment (15 minutes). (4) It uses 'role was eliminated' — the universally understood RIF language.

Who to reach out to (in order): (1) Former managers — they know your work quality. They may have roles on their new teams or know colleagues who are hiring. (2) Former teammates who left before the layoff — they are settled and may have leads. (3) People at companies that are hiring — Apple, NVIDIA, Microsoft, and non-FAANG tech companies that are expanding. (4) Recruiters you worked with before — they have a vested interest in placing you (commission). (5) People in your broader professional network — conference connections, LinkedIn 2nd-degree connections at target companies.

The LinkedIn strategy during a layoff: (1) Change your headline to remove the company name temporarily if it hurts to see it. (2) Change your 'Open to Work' setting to 'Recruiters only' (not the public green banner — that signals desperation). (3) Post once about your availability — not bitter, not begging. 'After 3 great years at [Company], my role was eliminated in the company RIF. I'm looking for my next Staff+ engineering opportunity. If you know of teams hiring, I'd appreciate an introduction.' (4) Engage with others' posts — comment professionally, share insights. Stay visible without being needy.

The downturn opportunity: Downturns create market dislocations that smart engineers exploit. When everyone is panicking, companies that are still hiring have less competition for top talent. Apple, NVIDIA, and Microsoft were actively hiring FAANG layoff engineers in 2023-2024. Some engineers got better offers (higher level, more equity) than they had before the layoff. The best time to negotiate is when you are already employed — the second-best time is when the market is down and you are a known quantity (FAANG Staff+ engineer) that companies are eager to scoop up.

sections.codeBlock:
# LinkedIn Announcement Template (Do NOT Post Before Week 3)

[Professional, not emotional. Short, not detailed.]

---

After [X years] at [Company], my role was eliminated
in the company's recent restructuring.

I'm proud of what I built — [1-sentence highlight of
key impact, e.g., 'led the migration of the payment
platform that now processes $X billion annually'].

I'm looking for my next Staff+ engineering role where
I can solve hard infrastructure and platform problems.

If you know of teams hiring, I'd appreciate an
introduction. Open to [types of roles/companies].

---

Do NOT include:
- Bitterness or blame ('bad management', 'wrong decision')
- Desperation ('willing to take anything', 'need a job ASAP')
- Salary or level requirements (save for private conversation)
- Company internal information (never share confidential details)

sections.title: When to Leave Before the Layoff — The Vest-and-Leave Decision

description.value:
The best layoff protection is leaving before the layoff happens. This sounds counterintuitive — why would you leave a job voluntarily during a downturn? Because the signals that a layoff is coming are often visible 3-6 months in advance, and leaving on your terms is better than being part of a RIF.

Signals it is time to leave before the layoff: (1) Your org has lost strategic priority — the CEO's all-hands doesn't mention your team's work. Budget cuts disproportionately affect your team. (2) Hiring freeze for your team specifically (not company-wide) — your team is not growing, which means it is being managed to shrink. (3) Leadership exodus — your Director, VP, or senior TL left. New leadership often brings their own people and deprioritizes existing teams. (4) Your project is winding down with no clear next project — if you have delivered your major initiative and there is no compelling next project, you are more likely to be cut. (5) The company has done multiple rounds of layoffs — companies that lay off once often do 2-3 rounds. The first round cuts obvious overcapacity; subsequent rounds cut deeper into core teams.

The vest-and-leave calculation: 'I have $X in unvested RSUs over the next Y months. If I leave now, I forfeit $Z. If I stay and get laid off, I may get accelerated vesting of some tranches. What is the expected value?' The formula: Expected value of staying = (Probability of layoff × Severance value) + (Probability of no layoff × Natural vesting) - (Probability of layoff × Career disruption cost). If the career disruption cost of being laid off (stigma, emotional toll, rushed job search) is high, and the unvested equity is small relative to your savings, leaving voluntarily may be the better choice.

The 'soft landing' strategy: If you see the signals, start looking for your next role before the layoff. Do not broadcast that you are looking. Be discreet. Use the 2-3 month window to: (1) Update your resume and LinkedIn quietly (do not use the 'Open to Work' setting). (2) Reach out to your network with: 'I'm not actively looking, but I'm curious about what opportunities exist. Would you be open to a chat?' This is true — until the layoff happens. (3) Interview at 2-3 companies. Do not accept an offer until you need to. (4) If the layoff hits before you have an offer, you have already done the groundwork. If it doesn't hit, you have market intelligence and optionally a competing offer to negotiate with your current employer.

The golden handcuffs trap: The biggest mistake SAP engineers make at FAANG is staying too long for the RSUs. 'I have $200K in unvested equity. I can't leave.' But if you are in a vulnerable org and a layoff is coming, you may lose both the equity AND the control over your transition. Unvested equity is not your money until it vests. Do not let the promise of future money trap you in a deteriorating situation. The question is not 'How much equity will I lose?' The question is 'If I were not working here, would I take this job today?' If the answer is no, leave — even if it costs you unvested equity.

sections.codeBlock:
# Vest-and-Leave Decision Calculator

def layoff_expected_value(
    unvested_equity,        # Total unvested RSUs in $
    next_vest_date,          # Days until next vest
    next_vest_value,         # Value of next vest in $
    prob_layoff,             # 0.0 to 1.0
    severance_months,        # Expected months of severance
    severance_monthly,       # Monthly severance in $
    career_disruption_cost,  # Cost of rushed search in $
    current_salary,          # Current monthly salary in $
    new_role_monthly         # Expected new role salary in $
):
    """
    Returns expected value of staying vs leaving voluntarily.
    """
    # Expected value of staying
    expected_severance = prob_layoff * severance_months * severance_monthly
    expected_vest = prob_layoff * next_vest_value + (1 - prob_layoff) * unvested_equity
    expected_disruption = prob_layoff * career_disruption_cost
    ev_stay = expected_severance + expected_vest - expected_disruption

    # Expected value of leaving now
    ev_leave = current_salary * 3  # ~3 months to find new role
    + current_salary * 12  # assume 1 year at new role
    # (simplified — actual calculation should include new salary)

    return ev_stay, ev_leave

# Example:
# Unvested: $300K
# Prob layoff: 40%
# Severance: 4 months at $30K/month = $120K
# Next vest: $50K (3 months away)
# Career disruption cost: $50K
# EV Stay = 0.4 * 120K + (0.4 * 50K + 0.6 * 300K) - 0.4 * 50K
#         = 48K + 200K - 20K = $228K
# EV Leave = need to compare to new role's total comp over same period

checklist.prompt: Should You Leave Before the Layoff? Signals Checklist

items.label: Your org/team has lost strategic priority in the last 6 months
Hiring freeze specific to your team (not company-wide)
Your Director or VP left in the last 3 months
Your major project is winding down with no clear next project
The company has done 1+ rounds of layoffs already
Budget cuts have affected your team's tools, travel, or contractors
You have 6+ months of living expenses in savings
Your network is warm — you could get referrals within 2 weeks

sections.title: The Re-Entry Strategy — From Layoff to Next Role

description.value:
The average FAANG Staff+ engineer laid off in 2022-2024 found their next role in 2-5 months. The range was wide: some engineers with niche skills or high levels (L7/8) took 6-9 months; others were scooped up within 2 weeks. Your goal is to be in the 2-3 month range by following a structured re-entry strategy.

Week 1-2: Foundation. Do not apply to jobs. Build the foundations: (1) Update your resume. Remove the bitterness. Every bullet should be quantifiable impact. Example: 'Led the migration of the payment platform serving $X billion annually, reducing P99 latency by 40%.' (2) Update LinkedIn. Add a professional 'open to work' note (see template above). (3) Update your brag doc — a running list of accomplishments and impact metrics from your FAANG tenure. (4) Identify target companies. List 10-15 companies. Include a mix: other FAANG (Apple, NVIDIA, Microsoft), top-tier tech (Stripe, Snowflake, Databricks), high-growth startups (Series C+), and enterprise companies that value FAANG experience (Workday, Salesforce).

Week 3-6: Active search. (1) Apply to 5-10 roles per week. Use referrals when possible — referrals increase interview rate by 3-5x. (2) Network: 5-10 informational interviews per week. Each call should end with: 'Based on our conversation, do you think I'd be a good fit for [role]? Would you be willing to refer me?' (3) Prepare for interviews. FAANG interviews are the same whether you are employed or not. Spend 5-10 hours per week on: system design review (your own designs from FAANG), behavioral stories (reframed for your next role), coding practice (LeetCode medium/hard). (4) Track everything in a spreadsheet: company, role, contact, stage, next step, date.

Week 7-12: Interview and decision. (1) Aim to have 3-5 active pipelines. Do not put all eggs in one basket. (2) When you get an offer, use it to accelerate other pipelines. 'I have an offer from [Company] with a deadline of [date]. I prefer [your Company]. Can we expedite the process?' (3) Evaluate offers holistically: base + equity + level + WLB + growth potential + team quality. A lower offer with better growth potential may be worth more in 2 years. (4) When you accept, negotiate the start date to give yourself 1-2 weeks between jobs. A transition break is not a weakness — it is a reset.

The SAP engineer's advantage in a downturn: SAP engineers are hired at FAANG for their enterprise experience, system-level thinking, and ability to navigate complex organizations. In a downturn, these skills become more valuable — FAANG companies need to optimize their enterprise products (which generate stable revenue) more than their consumer products (which depend on ad spending). When Meta laid off 25% of its workforce in 2023, it continued hiring enterprise sales and enterprise engineering roles. SAP engineers who position themselves as 'enterprise infrastructure experts' rather than 'FAANG generalists' have a natural advantage during tech downturns.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "I have a non-compete or non-solicit agreement. Can I even interview at competitors?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Non-competes are probably unenforceable in California. Don't worry about it." (oversimplified — gives dangerous false confidence)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Check three things: (1) Your state — California and Washington have effectively banned non-competes for most employees. If you worked in CA, your non-compete is likely unenforceable. (2) Your role — the higher your level and the more access you had to trade secrets, the more likely the company will enforce. L7+ engineers at Google who go to Meta often face legal scrutiny. (3) Your agreement — non-solicits (you cannot recruit former coworkers) are more enforceable than non-competes (you cannot work for a competitor). Send your agreement to an employment lawyer for a 30-minute review. The cost is $300-500 and gives you confidence to interview freely. Do not self-diagnose legal constraints — I've seen engineers lose offers because they assumed a non-compete was unenforceable and their target company's legal team flagged it."
adversarialResponse.pattern: legal-nuance — don't oversimplify non-compete enforcement. Give state-by-state guidance and recommend professional legal review. The cost of a lawyer is negligible compared to the cost of losing an offer or getting sued.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: If you are interviewing after a layoff, expect the question 'Why did you leave your last role?' Answer professionally: 'My role was eliminated as part of a company-wide restructuring. The team I was on was deprioritized, and the headcount was reduced. I'm proud of what I built there — [specific impact]. I'm looking for a role where I can [what you want next].' Do not bad-mouth the company. Do not show bitterness. Interviewers know about FAANG layoffs. They are testing whether you can talk about it professionally. The worst thing you can say is anything negative about your former employer — that signals that you will do the same to them. The best thing you can say shows resilience, professionalism, and forward focus.
